title: NOC 95107 – Labourers in fish and seafood processing | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s blue economy is thriving, with the seafood processing sector playing a vital role in global food security and export trade. Labourers in Fish and Seafood Processing (NOC 95107) are the frontline workers responsible for the initial handling, sorting, and packaging of Canada’s world-renowned maritime products. While classified as TEER 5, this occupation is a high-priority "In-Demand" role, especially in Atlantic Canada and British Columbia. For international workers, this NOC provides unique access to specialized immigration streams designed to address chronic labor shortages in the seafood industry.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category5 (Occupations usually requiring short work demonstration)Major Group95 – Labourers in processing, manufacturing and utilitiesSub-Major Group951 – Labourers in processing and manufacturing

duties: Unloading: Discharging fish and shellfish from fishing vessels and transporting them to work areas by hand or forklift.
Conditioning: Immersing fish fillets in brine solutions to prepare them for freezing or wrapping.
Sorting & Weighing: Categorizing fish by species, size, and destination; recording weights for production logs.
Packaging: Packing fish in ice or containers and preparing finished seafood products for global shipment.
Sanitization: Cleaning work areas and processing equipment to maintain strict 2026 Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) standards.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education may be required. No formal degree is necessary for entry-level roles.
Training: Short-term on-the-job training is provided.
Physical Demands: Ability to stand for long periods, perform repetitive motions, and work in cold, wet, and noisy environments.
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS certification and food safety orientation.
salary_prospects: Note: In regions with high seasonal demand, many employers offer additional completion bonuses and shift premiums.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates a "Balanced" market. While automation is being introduced for simple sorting, the high variation in seafood products ensures that human dexterity remains essential. Retirement and the expansion of aquaculture are expected to create over 5,000 new openings by 2033.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 labor market for seafood labourers is characterized by "Good" to "Very Good" prospects in coastal provinces:
Nova Scotia & New Brunswick: Very Good prospects. High demand to support the massive lobster, crab, and salmon export industries.
Prince Edward Island: Good prospects. The sector is a primary employer, with a constant need for seasonal and year-round support staff.
British Columbia: Good prospects. Focused in the Mainland/Southwest and Vancouver Island regions, particularly for salmon and shellfish processing.
Newfoundland and Labrador: Strong demand. Ongoing need for labourers to support the revitalization of traditional groundfish and shrimp processing plants.
Why Work in Canada as a Seafood Processing Labourer?
Priority Immigration Status: This NOC is frequently included in the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the Agri-Food Pilot, providing faster PR pathways.
Seasonal & Full-Time Flexibility: Options exist for both year-round positions and high-intensity seasonal work with significant overtime opportunities.
Essential Global Industry: Work in an industry that exports to over 130 countries, ensuring long-term sector stability and investment.
Growth Potential: Gain the experience needed to advance into NOC 94142 (Fish and Seafood Plant Workers) or NOC 92012 (Supervisors).
pathways: Agri-Food Pilot (Renewed for 2026): A specialized federal pathway for fish and seafood labourers with 12 months of Canadian experience.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): One of the most successful pathways for international workers with job offers in the Atlantic provinces.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated transition stream for 33,000 temporary residents. Seafood labourers are classified as "Essential Food Workers" and are high-priority candidates.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RCIP): A high-success pathway in coastal communities that rely on seafood processing for their local economy.
